1: Psalm 57 carries the superscription אַל-תַּשְׁחֵת (ʾal-tashḥêt, "Do Not Destroy") — a tune title appearing also in Psalms 58, 59, and 75. The historical note: David fleeing Saul and hiding in a cave (1 Samuel 22:1 — the cave of Adullam; or 1 Samuel 24:3 — the cave at En-Gedi). חָנֵּנִי אֱלֹהִים חָנֵּנִי (ḥonnênî ʾĕlōhîm ḥonnênî, "Be merciful to me, O God, be merciful to me!") — the doubled imperative intensifying the plea. בִּי חָסָיָה נַפְשִׁי (bî ḥāsāyāh napshî, "for my soul takes refuge in you") — ḥāsāh: to flee for refuge (the fundamental trust-verb of the Psalter). וּבְצֵל-כְּנָפֶיךָ אֶחְסֶה (ûbĕṣêl-kĕnāpêkā ʾeḥseh, "in the shadow of your wings I will take shelter") — ṣêl kĕnāpêkā: "the shadow of your wings" — the cherubim wings overshadowing the ark (Exodus 25:20; Psalms 37:9), or the divine mother-bird brooding over the young (Deuteronomy 32:11). The cave hides David physically; the divine wings shelter his nāpesh spiritually.
2: אֶקְרָא לֵאלֹהִים עֶלְיוֹן (ʾeqrāʾ lêʾlōhîm ʿelyôn, "I cry out to God Most High") — ʿElyôn: the Most High (the ancient divine title used by Melchizedek in Genesis 14:18-20 — the same ʿElyôn that Psalms 47:2 crowns as universal king). לָאֵל גֹּמֵר עָלָי (lāʾêl gōmêr ʿālay, "to God who completes all for me") — gāmar: to complete, to accomplish, to bring to an end (Psalms 138:8: "The LORD will fulfill/complete his purpose for me"). God is the one who brings his purposes to completion — not a God who leaves things half-done. The divine faithfulness expressed as completion of intention.
3: יִשְׁלַח מִשָּׁמַיִם וְיוֹשִׁיעֵנִי (yishlah mishshāmayim wĕyôshîʿênî, "He will send from heaven and rescue me") — the rescue dispatched from the heavenly throne — God's response to prayer crosses the cosmic distance between heaven and earth. חֵרַף שֹׁאֲפִי (ḥêrap shōʾăpî, "he will rebuke the one who tramples me") — the divine rebuke (ḥārap: to taunt, to reproach) directed back at the one who taunts the Psalmist. Selāh — pause on the divine action descending from heaven. יִשְׁלַח אֱלֹהִים חַסְדּוֹ וַאֲמִתּוֹ (yishlah ʾĕlōhîm ḥasdô waʾămittô, "God will send his steadfast love and his faithfulness") — ḥesed and ʾĕmet as the two divine envoys sent from heaven: covenant-loyalty and truth/faithfulness as personified agents dispatched to the Psalmist in the cave.
4: נַפְשִׁי בְּתוֹךְ לְבָאִים אֶשְׁכְּבָה לֹהֲטִים (napshî bĕtôk lĕbāʾîm ʾeshkĕbāh lōhăṭîm, "My soul is surrounded by lions; I must lie down among those who breathe fire") — lābiʾ: a lion (a poetic/archaic word for the lion — the great predator); lōhăṭîm: the fiery ones, those who devour with flame (from lāhaṭ: to blaze, to consume with fire — the divine fire of Exodus 3:2). The enemies are simultaneously lions and fire-breathers — a double predatory force. בְּנֵי-אָדָם שִׁנֵּיהֶם חֲנִית וְחִצִּים (bĕnê-ʾādām shinnêhem ḥănît wĕḥiṣṣîm, "human beings whose teeth are spears and arrows") — the mouth-as-weapon: teeth as spears, tongue as sword. The word-weapon cluster (also in Psalms 55:21: the sword-speech of the betrayer).
5: רוּמָה עַל-הַשָּׁמַיִם אֱלֹהִים (rûmāh ʿal-hashāmayim ʾĕlōhîm, "Be exalted above the heavens, O God!") — the refrain (repeated in v11 and identical to Psalms 108:5): rûm (imperative: be high, be exalted!). The prayer is directed at God himself: "Be exalted!" — not merely a statement that God is exalted but a cry for the manifestation of that exaltation. עַל כָּל-הָאָרֶץ כְּבוֹדֶךָ (ʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā, "let your glory be over all the earth!") — the universal scope: the kābôd of God filling the whole earth (as Isaiah 6:3: "the whole earth is full of his glory").
6: רֶשֶׁת הֵכִינוּ לִפְעָמַי (reshet hêkînû lipʿāmay, "They spread a net for my feet") — reshet: a hunting net (the trapper's snare for birds and animals). Pāʿam: a step, a footfall — the net spread at the point where the foot falls. כָּפַף נַפְשִׁי (kāpap napshî, "my soul was beaten down") — kāpap: to bow down, to be bent (the noun kippāh from the same root — the bowed head). כָּרוּ לְפָנַי שִׁיחָה נָפְלוּ בְתוֹכָהּ סֶלָה (kārû lĕpānay shîḥāh nāpĕlû bĕtôkāh selāh, "they dug a pit in my path, but they themselves have fallen into it") — shîḥāh: a pit (the hunters' trap). The reversal: the trap-diggers fall into their own trap — the ironic divine justice that appears throughout the Psalter (Psalms 35:8; Psalms 141:10).
7: נָכוֹן לִבִּי אֱלֹהִים נָכוֹן לִבִּי (nākhôn libbî ʾĕlōhîm nākhôn libbî, "My heart is fixed, O God, my heart is firm!") — kûn (niphal: to be established, to be set firm, to be prepared): the resolute inner stability after the storm of lament. The double repetition (nākhôn libbî... nākhôn libbî) is emphatic — the heart firmly established, not wavering. This is the inner reality that makes praise possible. אָשִׁירָה וַאֲזַמֵּרָה (ʾāshîrāh waʾăzammêrāh, "I will sing and make melody!") — the double response to the fixed heart: song (shîr) and instrumental praise (zāmar).
8: עוּרָה כְבוֹדִי עוּרָה הַנֵּבֶל וְכִנּוֹר (ʿûrāh kĕbôdî ʿûrāh hannêbel wĕkinnôr, "Awake, my honor! Awake, harp and lyre!") — ʿûr (imperative: awake, rouse!): the call to the inner self (kābôd = honor, the soul's dignity) and to the instruments. The harp (nêbel: a larger lyre-type) and the lyre (kinnôr: David's instrument — 1 Samuel 16:23). אָעִירָה שָּׁחַר (ʾāʿîrāh shāḥar, "I will rouse the dawn!") — the extraordinary claim: the Psalmist will wake the dawn with his praise — the pre-dawn rising before the sun. This reversal of the natural order (the dawn waking the worshipper) is replaced by the worshipper waking the dawn. The intensity of praise is such that human worship precedes the sunrise — not waiting for light but generating it through praise.
9: אוֹדְךָ בָעַמִּים אֲדֹנָי (ʾôdĕkā bāʿammîm ʾădōnāy, "I will give you thanks, O Lord, among the peoples") — ʿammîm: the peoples, the nations. The cave-bound fugitive resolves to praise among the international community — the witness to the nations as the goal of covenant survival. אֲזַמֶּרְךָ בַּלְאֻמִּים (ʾăzammĕrĕkā ballĕʾummîm, "I will sing your praise among the nations") — lĕʾummîm: ethnic communities, nations. Paul cites a version of this universal praise in Romans 15:9 (via Psalms 18:49): "Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles." The individual survival of the persecuted one becomes the occasion for universal witness.
10: כִּי-גָדֹל עַד-שָׁמַיִם חַסְדֶּךָ (kî-gādōl ʿad-shāmayim ḥasdekā, "For your steadfast love reaches to the heavens") — ḥesed measured in cosmic vertical distance: from earth to sky. וְעַד-שְׁחָקִים אֲמִתֶּךָ (wĕʿad-shĕḥāqîm ʾămittekā, "your faithfulness to the skies") — shĕḥāqîm: the gossamer clouds, the high heavens (from shāḥaq: to grind fine — the finest dust that rises highest). ʾĔmet (faithfulness/truth) reaching the very top of the atmosphere. The two divine attributes are not merely large — they are cosmically scaled: as high as the sky is from the earth.
11: The refrain repeated verbatim from v5: רוּמָה עַל-הַשָּׁמַיִם אֱלֹהִים עַל כָּל-הָאָרֶץ כְּבוֹדֶךָ — "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" The refrain closes the psalm as it opened the psalm's second half (v5): the prayer for divine exaltation and universal glory frames the personal experience of crisis and rescue. Note that Psalms 57:7-11 is almost identical to Psalms 108:1-5 — the early church's practice of assembling liturgical combinations from existing psalm material (a "scissors-and-paste" canonical creativity).
